chunk_summary: This section discusses macronutrient modifications and feeding methods in chronic pediatric diseases, focusing on disease-specific mechanisms like energy expenditure, malabsorption, and safe feeding strategies for growth and health maintenance.
learning_objectives: Understand how disease-specific mechanisms influence macronutrient needs in chronic pediatric conditions.
Learn safe feeding strategies for children with neurodisability, cystic fibrosis, Crohn’s disease, and cholestatic liver disease.
Recognize the importance of early nutrition screening and intervention during intensive oncology therapy.
key_facts: Children with cystic fibrosis often require 110–200% of standard energy needs after age 2, with a high-fat diet and pancreatic enzyme replacement.
Exclusive enteral nutrition can induce remission in pediatric Crohn’s disease and must be specialist-led.
In neurodisability with dysphagia, texture modification and caregiver feeding techniques are critical to ensure safe energy density.
Chronic cholestatic liver disease may require higher total fat intake with a major MCT component and fat-soluble vitamin monitoring.
During intensive oncology therapy, early nutrition screening and high-protein/high-calorie foods are essential to counter reduced intake.
reference_quote: In CF, energy needs are often markedly elevated (commonly 110–200% of standard after age 2); a high-energy, higher-fat pattern with specialist-directed pancreatic enzyme replacement is standard.
document_key_topics: Macronutrient modifications in chronic pediatric diseases
Energy and nutrient needs in cystic fibrosis, Crohn’s disease, and cholestasis
